one_line_takeaway: Carefully projected PGD on continuously relaxed tokens makes gradient-based LLM jailbreak attacks as strong as discrete GCG at up to 10x less compute, enabling large-scale white-box robustness evaluation and adversarial training.
key_finding: On the AdvBench-style 'behavior' jailbreaking task across six models, PGD reaches the same low target-probability / cross-entropy as GCG at up to an order of magnitude less runtime, while GBDA barely achieves meaningful target probability. On Vicuna 1.3 7B, a 60-second snapshot (Table 1, using Zou et al. template matching) gives PGD 87% ASR vs GCG 83% and GBDA 40%, with PGD running 28.2 iters/s vs GCG's 0.3 (GBDA 29.3), i.e. PGD nearly matches GBDA's cheap-per-iteration speed while being far more effective. The projections shrink the effective per-token search space from ~65,000 vocabulary entries to ~10. Ablation (Table 2, Vicuna): mean cross-entropy improves from 0.092 (neither) to 0.085 (variable length) to 0.078 (variable length + entropy projection); the authors attribute the largest gain over GBDA to the choice of relaxation itself, with flexible length and entropy projection giving further improvement. Discrete attacks like GCG typically need >100,000 LLM calls, which PGD avoids.
core_contribution: The paper revives ordinary gradient-based optimization for white-box adversarial attacks on aligned LLMs by adapting Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) to a continuously relaxed one-hot token representation. Its own novel contributions are: (1) a two-stage projection after each gradient step that keeps the relaxed tokens on the probabilistic simplex and then controls the relaxation error via an entropy (Gini-index) projection; (2) a flexible-length relaxation that smoothly inserts or removes tokens by learning a soft mask added to the causal attention mask, letting the attack optimize over a variable-length adversarial suffix/prefix; and (3) the first explicit analysis of the cost-effectiveness trade-off in automatic red teaming. The headline claim is that this PGD matches the attack strength of GCG's discrete optimization at up to one order of magnitude lower compute. Note that PGD itself in continuous/image domains (Madry et al. 2018), and the baselines GBDA (Guo et al. 2021) and GCG (Zou et al. 2023), are prior work, not this paper's invention; likewise the projection math is borrowed (Duchi et al. 2008 simplex projection; Tsallis 1988 entropy), while the entropy-projection *strategy* for opposing relaxation error is claimed as novel.
threat_model: White-box adversarial jailbreaking. The attacker is assumed to know the model parameters and architecture (needed to compute gradients w.r.t. the one-hot token encoding). Given a fixed prefix (system prompt plus an inappropriate user request) and a target harmful response, the attacker optimizes an adversarial suffix/prefix so the model emits an affirmative/harmful continuation, instantiated via cross-entropy on the target tokens. A second 'obedience' threat variant has the attacker force a Vicuna model to violate a system-prompt constraint ('not allowed to use the word <word>') by starting its reply with the forbidden word. The authors explicitly note the attack is not applicable to deployed black-box assistants (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) and did not test against them, framing the work as a tool for defenders (adversarial training, large-scale robustness evaluation).
limitations: The authors' own most important caveat: under a stronger judge-based ASR (HarmBench, Mazeika et al. 2024) rather than target-probability/template matching, GCG was found slightly superior in some cases, which they attribute to differing implicit optimization biases and a mismatch between the optimized objective and real attack success; a follow-up paper (Geisler et al. 2025) addresses this with an RL-based objective. Other limitations: strictly white-box (requires parameters and architecture; not applicable to deployed black-box assistants); discretization can require many iterations before a better discrete prompt is found, so finding discrete adversarial prompts remains harder than optimizing relaxed ones; the entropy projection does not guarantee a target entropy (only reaches it via repeated application), and enforcing entropy did not help. Evaluation is limited to six ~2B-8B open models on 100 prompts, and low-perplexity/stealth benefits of variable length are conjectured rather than measured.

datasets_used: 'Behavior' jailbreaking task of Zou et al. 2023 (AdvBench-style harmful-behavior prompts), 100 randomly selected prompts
Custom 'obedience' dataset of 100 words (fruit names) with a system prompt forbidding a given word
Models attacked: Vicuna 1.3 7B, Falcon 7B, Falcon 7B Instruct, Llama3 8B, Gemma 2B, Gemma 7B
quantitative_results: Vicuna 1.3 7B ASR @60s (Zou et al. template matching): PGD 87%, GCG 83%, GBDA 40% (Table 1)
PGD up to one order of magnitude faster than GCG at equal target-probability/cross-entropy (Fig. 3, main claim)
Ablation mean cross-entropy on Vicuna: 0.092 +/- 0.014 (no var length, no entropy proj); 0.085 +/- 0.010 (var length only); 0.078 +/- 0.009 (var length + entropy proj) (Table 2)
Per-token search space narrowed from ~65,000 to ~10 possibilities via projections (Falcon-7B-Instruct example, Fig. 5)
Obedience task: PGD reaches near-perfect success within 100 s and matches GBDA's performance 26x faster (Fig. 4)
Discrete-optimization baselines typically require >100,000 LLM calls (motivation)
Hardware/setup: single 40GB A100; 100 eval prompts; tuning on 50 Vicuna prompts, 1000 steps, 128-trial random search
baselines_compared: GCG (Zou et al. 2023) - gradient-assisted discrete optimization, strongest prior attack on robust LLMs
GBDA (Guo et al. 2021) - gradient-based distributional attack using Gumbel-Softmax relaxation
